chunks.title: Vehicle dynamics knowledge base (Engineer)
chunks.excerpt:
# Vehicle dynamics knowledge base (Engineer)

Markdown files in this folder are **searchable reference** for the Engineer chat. The app loads `.md` files (except this README), splits them on `##` headings, and ranks sections by **keyword overlap** with the user’s latest message.

chunks.title: Vehicle dynamics knowledge base (Engineer) — How to add content
chunks.excerpt:
1. Create a new `.md` file or add sections to an existing one.
2. Use `## Section title` for chunks the retriever can score independently.
3. Write **timeless** RC vehicle-dynamics material: cause → effect, what to try first, common mistakes.
4. Prefer **universal** language (touring car / on-road) unless the file is clearly labeled for a specific platform.
5. Keep sections under ~900 characters when possible so excerpts fit the model context cleanly.

chunks.title: Vehicle dynamics knowledge base (Engineer) — What not to put here
chunks.excerpt:
- User-specific setups, lap times, or secrets.
- Long copy-pastes from copyrighted books; summarize in your own words.

chunks.title: Vehicle dynamics knowledge base (Engineer) — Improving retrieval later
chunks.excerpt:
- Add frontmatter `tags:` per file for filtering (e.g. `grip`, `understeer`, `springs`).
- Replace keyword search with embeddings when you have a stable chunking strategy and evaluation set.

chunks.title: Vehicle dynamics knowledge base (Engineer) — Team / “what works” data
chunks.excerpt: Historical **spread** and **per-parameter position** for *your* cars come from `SetupParameterAggregation` in the database (rebuilt from your snapshots), not from this folder. Teammate-wide aggregates are a separate future product feature.

chunks.title: arm-angles-camber-gain — Lower link line — not the same “angled” as the upper
chunks.excerpt: **Geometry (absolute; keys in `roll-centre.md`):** In **side view**, a **more acute** line from the inner-lower to outer-lower pick-ups (smaller `under_lower_arm` and `under_hub` stacks, all else equal) **lowers roll centre** on that end. **Larger** inner-lower and outer-lower (hub) stacks **raise roll centre** and read as a **less acute (flatter) lower line**. The upper link’s “**flatter** / **more angled**” map is different: a **more angled upper** in the `roll-centre` sens

chunks.title: arm-angles-camber-gain — Camber in roll (camber gain)
chunks.excerpt:
**Geometry (absolute):** **Camber in roll** (how much the wheel **adds negative camber** as the suspension works in a corner) is set by the link and **roll centre** package on that end, not by static `camber_front` / `camber_rear` alone.

- A **flatter** upper link in the `roll-centre` **Flatter vs more angled** sense **decreases camber gain** in roll on that end.
- A **more angled** upper link in that same definition **increases camber gain** in roll on that end.
- **Hig

chunks.title: balance-and-grip — Grip, load transfer, and balance
chunks.excerpt:
**Grip** is limited by how much **lateral (or longitudinal) force** each tire can produce. **Load transfer** moves vertical load between tires; **unequally loaded tires** cannot add force in proportion to load at every instant, so the axle’s **usable** grip depends on how load is shared.

**Suspension and geometry stiffness** (roll stiffness, anti geometry, chassis flex where relevant) set how **fast** load moves when the driver steers or the car rolls: **stiffer** paths us

chunks.title: balance-and-grip — Response versus sustained grip
chunks.excerpt:
**Response** here means **lateral bite tied to quick load transfer** right after a steering input or as roll starts: the car **reacts** soon, with a **strong early peak** of side force if the tire and geometry allow it.

**Sustained grip** here means **lateral force that holds later** in the same corner maneuver—through mid-corner roll and steady curvature—often with a **smoother**, **more rolled-in** feel at the limit.

**Stiffer** roll control (springs, ARBs, geometry t

chunks.title: balance-and-grip — Mid-corner understeer
chunks.excerpt:
First separate **corner type** and **phase**: **hairpin / low-speed** vs **high-speed**, and whether the problem is truly **mid-corner** or partly **lack of response on entry** that the driver **reads** as push through the whole corner.

**Hairpins — sustained front limit:** If the front **stops building steering** mid-corner after a reasonable turn-in, the front may lack **sustained grip** relative to what the corner asks—often linked to the front being **too stiff** in ro

chunks.title: balance-and-grip — High-speed understeer and aero
chunks.excerpt:
At **high sustained lateral** load, **aero balance** matters strongly: if the **front aero** is weak **relative to the rear** for the speed and wing setup, the car can feel as if **mechanical balance changes barely move** the push—**more front downforce relative to the rear** (within rules and practicality) is often required for **more high-speed steering**, not only softer front springs.

**Damping** and **nose pitch** still matter (keeping the front loaded in long corners

chunks.title: bump-steer-toe-gain — Bump steer (front)
chunks.excerpt:
**Key:** `bump_steer_shims_front` — bump steer shimming on the **front** of the car.

**Convention (this platform):** **More shims → more bump-in** on compression. **Fewer shims → more bump-out** on compression. **Per-car setup sheets** document how many shims mean what for **your** chassis; do not assume the same count as another platform.

**Mechanics:** The **toe link** sets how toe changes with wheel travel. When the link is **angled more upward toward the wheel**, it

chunks.title: bump-steer-toe-gain — Toe gain (rear)
chunks.excerpt:
**Key:** `toe_gain_shims_rear` — toe gain shimming on the **rear** of the car.

**Convention (this platform):** **More shims → more bump-out / toe loss on compression**. **Fewer shims → more bump-in / toe gain on compression** (more rear **toe-in** as the wheel compresses). **Per-car setup sheets** define stack direction for your chassis.

**Mechanics:** Rear **toe gain** uses the same idea as front bump steer: the **toe link angle** sets how much the link **shortens or l

chunks.title: camber-caster-toe — Camber
chunks.excerpt:
**Keys:** `camber_front`, `camber_rear` — camber is the **tilt of the tire relative to the ground** (angle in the wheel plane), usually measured static at ride height; **roll in cornering** changes the tire’s **effective** angle to the road. **Camber in roll** (camber **gain** from **links** and **roll centre**—not the same as these static values) is in **`arm-angles-camber-gain.md`**.

**Mechanics:** **More negative camber** (top of tire inward) can help the **outside tire

chunks.title: camber-caster-toe — Caster (front vs rear)
chunks.excerpt:
**Keys:** `caster_front`, `caster_rear` — **front and rear caster behave very differently**; do not treat them as the same knob.

**Front caster — mechanics:** Caster **angles the path** the wheel follows when steered. From the **side view**, with **zero caster** the wheel moves mostly **forward and back** in steer; with **caster**, the same steer adds a **small up-and-down** component to the wheel’s travel—how much depends on **kingpin / steering-axis geometry** on your ca

chunks.title: camber-caster-toe — Front toe
chunks.excerpt:
**Key:** `toe_front` — many **touring** setups are described with **front toe-out** (not toe-in). **Less toe-out** (closer to parallel, or “**more toe-in**” if you cross zero) tends to **increase response on turn-in**. **More toe-out** tends to **calm** or **delay** initial response. **Sheet numbers and signs differ by template** — confirm whether a **larger magnitude** on your sheet means **more** or **less** toe-out before comparing to other drivers.

**Bump steer** chang

chunks.title: camber-caster-toe — Rear toe
chunks.excerpt: **Key:** `toe_rear` — **more rear toe** (typically **more rear toe-in** in the usual convention) usually **increases rear grip** at the cost of **rotation** and often **corner speed** — a lot of rear toe can **hurt minimum corner speed** noticeably. It often feels **safer and easier**, especially **mid-corner to exit**. **Less rear toe** usually gives **more rotation** and **less rear grip**; the car is often **harder to drive** and less forgiving. **Extra rear toe** can add

chunks.title: damper-oil — Damper oil (thicker vs lighter)
chunks.excerpt:
**Keys:** `damper_oil_front`, `damper_oil_rear` — **thicker oil** increases **damping** for a given piston / build (exact feel depends on **pistons, holes, and spring rate**).

**Thicker oil (front and rear)** usually feels **less reactive**, **easier to drive**, and often **better over bumps** / rough track — the car **moves less** on quick inputs and surface hits.

**Thicker front oil** tends to **calm initial steering** and **remove some mid-corner rotation** while fee

chunks.title: droop-downstop-arb — Droop and downstop (wheel travel)
chunks.excerpt:
**Keys:** `droop_front`, `droop_rear`, `downstop_front`, `downstop_rear` — sheets differ in whether **droop** (allowed extension) and **downstop** (travel limit) are separate or combined; read the **column label** on your setup. **Convention here:** a **lower number** with **more droop** means the car can **extend the wheels further** while still keeping **tire contact** over bumps and in roll (more available **extension** travel).

**Front:** **More front droop** (lower nu

chunks.title: droop-downstop-arb — ARB / sway bar (front)
chunks.excerpt: **Key:** `arb_front` — **softer** front ARB / **thinner** bar: often **more mid-corner steering**, especially in **hairpins**; can **smooth** initial front grip and feel **less grabby** / easier to drive. **Stiffer** front ARB: often **more initial bite** and **more steering in the first part** of the corner, then **more push / understeer** **later** in the same corner as the front loads up.

chunks.title: droop-downstop-arb — ARB / sway bar (rear)
chunks.excerpt: **Key:** `arb_rear` — rear ARB is often **less predictable** than front. **Stiffer** rear can **add rotation under high load**, especially **on throttle**, but **too stiff** can **remove rotation in hairpins** because the **rear cannot roll enough** to work the tire. **Softer** rear can sometimes give **more mid-corner grip**, but also **less stability under high load**; it can **add rotation in hairpins** depending on balance. **Test** and match to surface grip and driving s

chunks.title: flex-chassis — Flex (general)
chunks.excerpt: **More flex** usually makes the car **more forgiving** and can feel like **more overall grip**, especially when **track grip is low**. When **grip is high**, **too much flex** can feel like **less grip**: **vague**, lacking **support** and **precision**. Flex interacts with **springs, dampers, and geometry** — treat changes as part of a **whole car** balance.

chunks.title: flex-chassis — Front vs rear flex (handling)
chunks.excerpt: **More front flex** tends to **calm initial steering** / feel **smoother on turn-in**, and can add **mid-corner steering** as the nose loads. **More rear flex** often **increases rotation**, especially **on throttle**. **Less rear flex** often **improves rear grip** and **reduces rotation** — but if the rear is **too stiff**, some drivers feel **more rotation on throttle** (unload / snap) depending on diff, springs, and surface. **Test on track.**

chunks.title: flex-chassis — Chassis material (carbon vs steel vs RAF)
chunks.excerpt:
**Key:** `chassis` — options vary by kit (e.g. carbon, steel, RAF-style plates).

**Carbon** is usually the **softest** chassis flavour: often the **most overall grip** in **low-grip** conditions and can be the **easiest** when grip is **low** or the surface is **bumpy / inconsistent**. **Steel** typically **lowers CoG** and has **less flex** than carbon; drivers often use **softer top decks** or other flex tools to **reintroduce** compliance. **RAF / stiff alloy-type** pla

chunks.title: flex-chassis — Top deck: C127 vs C127S (example)
chunks.excerpt: **Keys:** `top_deck_front`, `top_deck_rear` — common presets include **C127** and **C127S** (names from setup sheets). **C127** is often **slightly wider** and therefore **stiffer** than **C127S** in many kits; use your **manual** for the exact part you run.

chunks.title: flex-chassis — Top deck screws and motor mount screws (stiffness)
chunks.excerpt:
**Keys:** `top_deck_screws`, `motor_mount_screws` — more screws engaged usually means **more stiffness** in that zone.

- **Top deck screws A–B** (front pair): adding screws here typically adds **front** stiffness.
- **Top deck screws E–F** (rear pair): adding screws here typically adds **rear** stiffness.
- **Motor mount screws** positions **1–2**: often associated with **front** stiffness; positions **3–4–5** with **rear** stiffness (layout is kit-specific — match label

chunks.title: flex-chassis — Top deck cuts (flex)
chunks.excerpt:
**Key:** `top_deck_cuts` — cuts usually **add flex** in the region of the deck.

- Cuts **A–D** (front half of the deck grid): usually **front flex**.
- Cuts **E–H** (rear half): usually **rear flex**.

Use the **same A–H grid** your PDF / app uses; do not assume letters match another brand’s deck.

chunks.title: response-vs-sustained-grip — Response versus sustained grip — what you are choosing
chunks.excerpt: **Response** is how hard the car bites **on entry**: peak reaction to steering, a **high peak** of side force then often a **drop** as roll builds. **Sustained grip** is how **consistent** lateral force feels **through** the corner: smoother, more **constant**, the car **slower to react** and often easier to read at the limit. Geometry (upper link, RC height, hub trim) shifts you along this spectrum together with tires and surface—use this as a map, not a guarantee.

chunks.title: response-vs-sustained-grip — Low RC and strong sustained grip — entry and feel
chunks.excerpt: A car with **low roll centre** and a lot of **sustained** grip tends to **enter corners smoothly**—sometimes **too smooth**: it can feel **imprecise** or vague if you want a sharp attack. It rewards a **rolled-in**, **in the track** style rather than a snappy stab.

chunks.title: response-vs-sustained-grip — High RC and strong response — entry and aggression
chunks.excerpt: A car with **high roll centre** and a lot of **response** **enters aggressively**: very reactive, can feel **harder to drive** because it is **too sharp** early. You get a **peak** of bite early, then the familiar **mid-corner** trade-offs from higher RC (see **Per end** theory in `roll-centre.md`).

chunks.title: response-vs-sustained-grip — Mid-corner — sustained versus response personality
chunks.excerpt: **Low RC** with **sustained** grip: the car **maintains grip** mid-corner, **rotates by rolling**, and often feels **safe** and **in the track**. **High response** / higher-RC character: rotation can come from **letting go of rear grip**—can feel **edgy**, **catch bumps**, or **want to flip / tip** if you overdrive (jacking and bump sensitivity matter).

chunks.title: response-vs-sustained-grip — Corner exit — fishtail vs drive away
chunks.excerpt: With a lot of **sustained** grip and **low RC** tendency, **exit** can **fishtail** or feel **unstable and imprecise**—like the car **won’t want to drive straight** on throttle. **High RC** with strong **response** often **wants to go straight off the corner** with good bite; it can also **snap** or **lose grip quickly** if **early** bite is too much or timing is wrong.

chunks.title: response-vs-sustained-grip — Under-hub shims — trimming response versus sustained grip
chunks.excerpt: **Keys:** `under_hub_shims_front`, `under_hub_shims_rear`. After upper link and inner lower arm, use hub height to trim: **lower** hub stack → more **response**; **higher** stack → more **sustained grip** (see `roll-centre.md`). Combine with RC balance—**response vs sustained grip** is not the same as **front vs rear** balance, but both matter together.

chunks.title: roll-centre — Roll centre basics
chunks.excerpt: Roll centre (RC) height is a core geometry choice; the whole setup is built around it. A useful **first** split is **on the track** vs **in the track** (see below)—then use parameters to move between them.

chunks.title: roll-centre — On the track vs in the track (driving style)
chunks.excerpt:
**On the track:** The car feels **responsive and reactive**, somewhat **high**, like it can **roll**; you often **calm** steering and throttle so it does not feel too nervous. Linked to **higher RC** and a **more angled** upper link (see **Flatter vs more angled**). You drive **within** the car a bit more.

**In the track:** The car feels **low**, **rolling down**, often **safer and smoother**; you can **push harder** and get on throttle with more confidence. Linked to **lo

chunks.title: roll-centre — Upper link: flatter vs more angled
chunks.excerpt:
**Flatter** upper link line (qualitatively closer to parallel with the lower link in side view): move toward **higher upper inner** *or* **lower upper outer**—either end can flatten the line.

**More angled** upper link: **lower upper inner** *or* **higher upper outer** (the opposite moves at each end).

Always judge **net** inner + outer together; do not treat one pair as the whole angle.

chunks.title: roll-centre — Theory preface — upper link balance
chunks.excerpt: The **next sections** are **theoretical**: they describe **what can or should** happen when you change **front vs rear** upper-link geometry and roll-centre balance. **Real feel does not always match**—grip, tires, bumps, dampers, and the rest of the car matter. **You must test on track**; use this as a map, not a guarantee.

chunks.title: roll-centre — Upper link balance (front vs rear)
chunks.excerpt: **Upper link balance** is the **relative** upper-link angle (and RC) **front vs rear**—many drivers treat it as the **fundamental** balance of the car. You adjust it by changing **front** upper link geometry **or** **rear**, **keeping the other end** similar, or moving **front and rear in opposite directions**. That shifts **roll-centre balance** between the axles—not just one corner in isolation.

chunks.title: roll-centre — One axle upper link changes — balance vs the other end
chunks.excerpt: If the diff changes **front** upper-link parameters (`upper_inner_shims_ff`/`fr` and/or `upper_outer_shims_front`) but **does not** change **rear** upper-link keys (`upper_inner_shims_rf`/`rr`, `upper_outer_shims_rear`), the **other** axle’s upper link is **unchanged vs compare**. You are moving **roll-centre balance**: the front’s RC and link angle **relative to** the rear—not only “what the front does” in isolation. Typical pattern when **raising front upper inner** (compar

chunks.title: roll-centre — Per end: flatter vs more angled (theory)
chunks.excerpt:
**The end with the less angled (flatter) link and lower RC** tends to: **less initial grip** on entry, **more grip mid-corner**; feels **more in the track** and often **handles bumps better**.

**The end with the more angled link and higher RC** tends to: **more initial grip** on entry, a **loss of grip mid-corner**, then **more again** as you **get on power** out of the corner; feels **stiffer, more on the track**, and often **worse on bumps**.

chunks.title: roll-centre — Flatter front and opposite front/rear (theory)
chunks.excerpt: A **flatter front** upper link often gives **smoother entry** and can **maintain grip later** into the corner. The **opposite** idea—**more angle / lower link at the front** and **flatter / higher at the rear**—can make the **front sharp into** the corner, then **give up a bit of front** mid / exit. If the balance goes **too far** that way and the **rear is too soft in roll**, you can **overload the rear** on throttle: **fishtail**, the car **won’t want to go straight** off t

chunks.title: roll-centre — Upper inner and upper outer shims — RC (canonical keys)
chunks.excerpt:
**Keys:** `upper_inner_shims_ff`, `upper_inner_shims_fr`, `upper_inner_shims_rf`, `upper_inner_shims_rr`, `upper_outer_shims_front`, `upper_outer_shims_rear`.

**This platform:** **Raising** the **upper inner** stack **lowers** RC on that corner (moves toward **in the track** in the sense above). **Lowering** **upper inner** **raises** RC (moves toward **on the track**). **Upper outer** changes the link line too—combine with inner for **net** flatter vs angled and **net** R

chunks.title: roll-centre — Upper outer — raise vs lower (net with inner)
chunks.excerpt: **Lowering** **upper outer** shims contributes to a **flatter** upper link at that end (see **Flatter vs more angled**). A **flatter** link there **tends toward lower RC** on that axle, not higher. **Raising** **upper outer** contributes to a **more angled** link and **tends toward higher RC**. Do not describe “flatter” as “higher roll centre”—that is backwards. On each end, state **net** inner + outer together before inferring handling.

chunks.title: roll-centre — Lower arm — under lower arm shims (not upper inner)
chunks.excerpt:
**Keys:** `under_lower_arm_shims_ff` … `under_lower_arm_shims_rr`. These move the **inner pickup of the lower arm** (chassis side)—**not** the upper inner shims. They **raise or lower RC** on that corner by that lower pivot; state RC effects as **inner lower arm** or **under lower arm**, never as “inner” alone.

**Convention:** **Raising** this stack **raises** RC on that corner; **lowering** it **lowers** RC. Pair with upper-link changes when you change the whole triangle.

chunks.title: roll-centre — Axle height (under hub — outer lower shims)
chunks.excerpt:
**Keys:** `under_hub_shims_front`, `under_hub_shims_rear`. **Outer lower** = shims **under the hub**. Use for **response vs sustained grip** trim after upper link and inner lower arm. **Lower** hub stack → more **response**; **higher** stack → more **sustained grip**.

For **Step 4** — **response vs sustained grip** personality **by corner phase** (entry / mid / exit), see **`response-vs-sustained-grip.md`** in this folder.

chunks.title: roll-centre — High roll centre — jacking and loose tracks
chunks.excerpt: High RC adds **jacking** (car lifts in roll); overdriving can **jack up** or flip. On **loose** surfaces the extra bite can help **response**—with less margin if you overdrive.

chunks.title: roll-centre — Low roll centre — lazy and fishtail caveat
chunks.excerpt: Low RC is more **in the track** in feel; on **very low grip** the car can feel **lazy**; under power the rear may **fishtail** if the balance is too soft for the surface.

chunks.title: roll-centre — Low front RC, high rear RC — handling
chunks.excerpt: Smooth initial steering, good mid-corner rotation, rear **supports** on power while the front may **push** a little—often **forgiving**, lower flip risk.

chunks.title: roll-centre — High front RC, low rear RC — handling
chunks.excerpt: Sharp into the corner, less mid-corner steer, rotation from the **rear**; on power do not **overload** a soft rear—**fishtail** possible on **loose** tracks. Can be very fast but trickier.

chunks.title: roll-centre — RC balance: order of adjustments
chunks.excerpt: Tune **net** geometry: **upper inner + upper outer**, **under lower arm**, **under-hub** shims, link length. You mainly set **front/rear RC balance** with **upper link** and **inner lower arm** front and rear, then trim **response vs sustained grip** with hub and link length. You can later move **both** ends together to shift **on vs in the track** without changing the **front/rear relationship** as much.

chunks.title: spring-rate — Spring rate (general)
chunks.excerpt:
**Keys (examples):** `spring_front`, `spring_rear`, `spring_gap_front`, `spring_gap_rear`, `front_spring_rate_gf_mm`, `rear_spring_rate_gf_mm` — your sheet may use **preset springs**, **gap**, or **rate (gf/mm)**; they all change **effective spring rate** at that end.

**Higher spring rates** generally give **more initial grip**, a **quicker-reacting** car, **more initial steering**, and **more initial rear grip** — until the tire is overloaded. **Surface grip, speed, and d

chunks.title: spring-rate — Front spring (stiffer vs softer)
chunks.excerpt: **Stiffer front spring** often behaves **similar to a stiffer front ARB** in feel: **more initial steering** and a **more reactive** front end, but often **less mid-corner steering** at **low speeds / low lateral load** (e.g. tight hairpins). At **high speed / high load**, a stiffer front can feel like **more steering** in general because the platform stays flatter. **Net:** many drivers report **less mid-corner rotation / oversteer** when they go too stiff on the front. **So

chunks.title: spring-rate — Rear spring (stiffer vs softer)
chunks.excerpt: **Stiffer rear spring** often **reduces mid-corner rotation**; can **improve on-throttle steering** (or **rear stability**) at **high load** depending on balance. Many drivers feel the car **squares up** on exits and **wants to go straight** sooner. **Too soft** can feel **vague** and not want to **drive straight off** the corner. **Stiffer rear** often feels like **more rear grip** in many conditions but **less compliant** and sometimes **harder to drive**. **Softer rear** c

chunks.title: support-lower-inner — Support — inner lower arm vs springs and ARB
chunks.excerpt: **Support** here means how firmly geometry carries lateral load and transfer—not only **roll centre** height, but **how forces run from the tire into the chassis**. The **inner lower pickup** (**under lower arm** shims, keys `under_lower_arm_shims_*`) is often the strongest lever for that **fundamental** character: it changes RC **and** the stiffness of the **kinematic** response. **Springs** and **ARB** change roll resistance too, but they do not scale the same way with corn

chunks.title: support-lower-inner — Raising the lower arm — RC, support, and load
chunks.excerpt: **Raising** the inner lower stack **raises roll centre** on that corner and adds **support**: the car feels **stiffer**, more held up at the limit. That extra support tends to track **load**—as grip and forces go up, geometric support rises with them. Tuning only **spring** or **ARB** can mask a lack of support until **grip or pace** increases; then you may chase the same problem again. When you need a durable change to how the car **supports** itself, **inner lower arm** geo

chunks.title: support-lower-inner — Rear lower arm too low — soft at the limit
chunks.excerpt: When **rear** inner lower is **too low**, the car often feels **soft at the limit**: the rear never quite **platforms** under power or in sustained lateral load. You can add **spring**, **ARB**, or damping to stiffen feel, but if **grip** or **forces** step up, you can end up fighting for **support** repeatedly. **Raising** the **rear** lower inner pickup addresses **rear support** at the source. Much of this “support” language in practice refers especially to the **rear** ax

chunks.title: support-lower-inner — Rear lower arm too high — over-support
chunks.excerpt: If the **rear** lower arm is **too high**, you can have **too much support**: the car feels **stiff** and **snappy**, rolls little, and **mid-corner** the **rear** may **lose grip** or feel nervous—it will not **breathe** in roll the way a balanced car does. The opposite problem to “too soft”: you are **over-supported** geometrically. **Lowering** the rear inner lower stack (within sensible range) reduces that geometric stiffness and can restore roll and rear grip mid-corner—

chunks.title: support-lower-inner — Front inner lower arm — support and handling (theory)
chunks.excerpt: **Raising** the **front** inner lower pickup **raises front roll centre** and adds **geometric support at the front** in the same sense as elsewhere: how lateral load is carried **kinematically**, not only via spring and ARB. Much casual **“support”** talk still centres on the **rear** (platform, throttle, fishtail)—but a **front** under–lower-arm change is still a **real** support + RC move; describe it as **front** support when the diff is **front** keys, not by borrowing *
